sections.title: a. Sliding Fee Assessment
sections.content:
FPCSN policy, approved by the Board, is that all required primary, preventive, dental, behavioral health, and additional health services are available and accessible to the target population without regard to patients' ability to pay, and that the health center has a sliding fee discount scale geared to U.S. poverty guidelines. Procedures dictate screening is conducted to determine all patients' eligibility for services under the sliding scale during the initial intake and annually. FPCSN ensures that patients are made aware of the sliding fee discount program by providing information on the website, signs in each site, and notifying patients during appointment scheduling and registration. To assess their eligibility for discounted fees, patients are asked to provide their family size and income information. Patients applying for the sliding fee scale discount program must present income documentation to determine income level that includes: previous year's tax return, current pay stub, pension check stub, beneficiary award

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
sections.content: letter (Social Security benefits, etc.), or personal attestation of earnings for people with no tax liability and/or who are self-employed. If patients do not have proper documentation with them, they are provided time to provide it for the discount to be applied to the visit. Once a discount has been approved, it is honored for a year, when the patient must reapply. Patients have the right to refuse to complete the assessment; they will be ineligible for discounts.

sections.title: b. How Patient Charges are Adjusted Based on Ability to Pay
sections.content:
FPCN has sliding fee scales for Primary Care, Behavioral Health, Integrated Behavioral Health, and Dental services. The FPCSN sliding scales are revised annually and approved by the Board, include three categories between 100% and 200% of federal poverty level, and are used for all clinical services. (See the sliding scale in Attachment 10) The scales and procedures are on hand for staff who register patients and they receive training in how charges are adjusted according to health center policy. The scales are embedded within the EHR to automatically calculate the discounts once family size and income are entered. Attempts are made to collect discounted fees, but patients are not refused care as a result of non-payment. Hence, no one is denied services due to their inability to pay.
c Sliding Fee Schedule Structure and Nominal Fees
There are $20 nominal charges in place for patients with income below 100% of poverty for primary care services, which can be waived in some cases. These nominal charges are flat charges, they are not set at the actual cost of the service provided, and they are set at a level that is nominal from the perspective of the patient.

sections.title: d. Evaluation of Sliding Fee Discount Program
sections.content: FPCSN policy requires that the Board reviews, revises as needed, and approves the policy and supporting operating procedures, including the schedule of charges, and those regarding the billing and collections of patient accounts, on an annual basis. This review is designed to determine that, from the perspective of the patient, the sliding fee scales do not create barriers to service. The Board uses patient satisfaction surveys for this evaluation and seeks input from patient members on the Board and from the Community Advisory Committees regarding potential barriers the fees might pose for patients.

children.title: 6. Care that is Respectful of and Responsive to Individual Needs
children.content:
a/b. Engagement of Patients in Care and Involvement with Families and Caregivers FPCSN services aim to improve patients' quality of life as defined by the individuals or families being served. Including families and caregivers in care, if the patient consents, is shown to increase health outcomes not only for patients but for families as well. It is critical that families are involved when appropriate to assist in strengthening the family unit through the services we provide. Goals for physical health, behavioral health, and meeting health related social needs are set with the patient and family, with a focus on what is important to them. Through discussion, providers and staff assure they fully understand the treatment options available and then that they understand details of the treatment regimen that is decided upon. If there are health-related social barriers to patients' receiving or implementing their treatment, such as needing to travel to a specialist, connect with food as medicine, or participate in exercise, referrals are made to assist patients with these needs. In some cases, behavioral health services are needed to help overcome barriers as well. Providers and all other staff have received training that helps them to be sensitive to the difficulties many patients have experienced that affect their ability to connect to services.

c. Alignment with the Needs of the Underserved Area Population
sections.content:
Since its inception over 30 years ago, the health center has been focused on increasing high quality integrated health care for those residents of the service area who are underserved as a result of their low-income status. FPCSN uses a number of ways to be sure that services are aligned with the needs of the communities served across Northwest, North, and Southwest Philadelphia and nearby Delaware County, areas experiencing high poverty, chronic disease, and limited access to care. Serving more than 15,000 patients annually, FPCSN provides comprehensive, integrated services including primary care, behavioral health, dental, perinatal, and enabling services that address social determinants of health.
Formal needs assessments are done at least every three years, as well as when a new service or site is proposed either internally or by an external partner such as the Housing Authority, the PHA Tenants' Council, or the School District. Guided by its Strategic Plan and CQI framework, FPCSN uses population data, UDS metrics, and health screenings to identify gaps in care and target resources. FPCSN's Board of Directors, which includes community representatives, reviews outcome data to ensure operations remain responsive and equitable. There are a majority of patient members on the Board who provide valuable information about how the health center is doing in serving the community and each site has a Community Advisory Committee that helps the director and staff to keep in touch with community residents and partners and their ongoing needs. Through these coordinated strategies, FPCSN advances principles of accessibility and focus on community needs, ensuring all patients, regardless of income or background, receive high-quality and responsive care.

sections.title: a. Communication Tools
sections.content: FPCSN is committed to ensuring that information on patient hospitalizations, including emergency department visits, is obtained and reviewed in a timely manner. The health center is part of the HealthShare Exchange (HSX) that provides the health center with real-time notice about health center patients who have been admitted to and discharged from participating hospitals for inpatient stays and emergency department visits. Staff have access to visit summaries that include prescription information and testing results, as well as the discharge summaries of FPCSN patients through area health systems electronic records. The health center works with area hospitals and other health providers in an integrated model of care, in that there are HIPAA-compliant arrangements for these providers to directly access the FPCSN EHR. Through the EHR, FPCSN staff receive information from the hospitals including lab and diagnostic results, and emergency care notes. Also, through the Azara population health management tool and the Aledade Accountable Care Organization FPCSN is a part of, the health center receives notifications of hospital and ER utilization that the Care Coordinators manage.

sections.title: b. Hospital Admitting
sections.content:
FPCSN works with the hospitals in the service area to assure patients referred by the health center will be admitted. All of the hospitals offer emergency and diagnostic care and a broad range of medical specialties and services and most are teaching hospitals that have affiliations with local universities. A formal agreement is in place with the Hospital of the University of Pennsylvania for referred patients to be admitted and have their care managed by hospitalists. Obstetric patients are accepted for inpatient delivery as part of the contracts in place with the Family Medicine group whose contract providers attend those patients and conduct medical rounds. These arrangements

In all cases, information about patients' treatment and needs upon discharge is communicated to health center staff.
c. Receipt, Follow-up, Recording of Medical Information from Referral Sources
FPCSN staff receive information from the hospitals when patients visit the emergency department or are hospitalized directly into the EHR via the HSX HealthShare Exchange and via fax from the payers. Summaries are imported directly into the patient's chart by the Exchange and medications lists are updated. Staff then have access to visit summaries that include prescription information and testing results, as well as the discharge summaries of these FPCSN patients.

sections.title: d. Health Center Follow-up for Hospitalizations or ED Visits
sections.content: Most of the area hospitals send lab and diagnostic results, as well as emergency care notes, directly into the EHR. When patients are scheduled for discharge, they are given instructions regarding their medications, limitations on physical activity, and dates for follow-up care at the FPCSN and health center care management staff is notified electronically so that transition to outpatient care can be facilitated. An FPCSN care coordinator at each site follows up on all patients who have had emergency room visits or hospitalizations to ensure there is a follow up plan in place to see their FPCSN provider or a specialist, that they understand and are taking their medication, and that they have a support system in place from family or community nursing services. Social service staff makes referrals as needed to a variety of community services, such as in-home services and Meals-on-Wheels, to provide that support for patients in the community.

children.title: 8. New Applicant Plan to Limit Disruption
children.content: As noted above, the health center has been providing health care services in the service area for 33 years. Because the previous awardee changed ownership and relinquished the grant on 6/30/25, the FPCSN health center has had to establish a new non-profit corporation and apply as a new applicant for the area it has served for many years. FPCSN has been able to keep all main sites open while providing all required services in order to limit disruption and this SAC application is being submitted to ensure that patients continue to have access to the services FPCSN provides. FPCSN has gathered tremendous support from the community. Understanding it will be some time before the health center again has FQHC status and supports, the Penn hospital system, a major health-focused foundation, the area insurers, and the state are all supporting and assisting the health center through this period in order to maintain FPCSN services and support our patients.

sections.title: COLLABORATION
sections.content:
1. Collaboration with Partners to Increase Awareness of Community Health Needs Family Practice & Counseling Services Network has been a part of the fabric of healthcare in the Philadelphia area for many years. As noted above, FPCSN has strong working relationships with other health care, education, and service providers in the Philadelphia area to provide for sufficient and seamless services to patients and assure that there is awareness of the community's health and health-related needs. FPCSN has working relationships with a number of the health care and social service providers in the area, including collaboration with HIV/AIDS and infectious disease clinics and other Ryan White funded services, including Action Wellness, Mazzoni Health Center, Philadelphia FIGHT FQHC, and Neighborhoods United Against Drugs.

sections.content:
The Abbottsford/Falls Health Center maintains an active presence in the Abbottsford Homes Housing Development. In addition to serving as a medical home for many residents, staff members work with the Philadelphia Housing Authority's community coordinators and partner organizations to provide community-based programming, including youth groups and events like the Holiday Drive. Abbottsford/Falls maintains relationships with local organizations to provide services to the community, including SOWN - a grandparents' resource and support group, Bridges to Wealth - a financial education program, Nice Roots Farm at the Share Food Program - fresh fruits and vegetables at affordable prices, the Food Trust, and Cradles to Crayons. The health center also has relationships with the co-located Caring Together substance abuse treatment program and the nearby Y's Pre-Diabetes program.
The 11th Street Family Health Services Center has always been very involved in the public housing neighborhood around it. The 11th Street site has also established partnerships and collaborations with the following: the John F. Street Community Center, Harrison Community Center, Fairmount Unit Boys & Girls Club, Columbia/North YMCA, St. Paul's Church Community Center, Better Tomorrows and West Poplar Apartments Community Center, International Ambassadors Mission, Mural Arts Program/Porch Light, South Kensington Community Partners, St. Christopher's Foundation's Farms to Families program, the Nurse Family Partnership, Gladys B. Jacobs Manor, FNC, PlainTalk Philadelphia, Temple University's Fox Chase Cancer Center, and the Ramonita de Rodriguez branch of the Free Library of Philadelphia, local Registered Community Organizations (RCO's), Treatment Research Institute, GreensGrow Farms, and Greener Partners community gardens.
Health Annex managers and staff are members of the Southwest Advisory Group, attend the Southwest Community Development Corporation board meetings, and are part of the 12th Police District monthly meetings. The Health Annex has a number of MOUs with area organizations that outline reciprocal referral relationships to provide comprehensive services to patients, including the Bartram Village public housing development, African Family Health Organization, Maternity Care Coalition, Paschallville Library, the Penn School of Nursing, Southwest Senior Center, Ezekiel Baptist Church, and The Village. The Health Annex extends itself to community organizations who need community space for meetings.

children.title: 2. Efforts to Collaborate and Coordinate Services with Other Providers
children.content:
FPCSN has strong collaborative relationships with the area hospitals, including the University of Pennsylvania Health System and Penn Medicine, the other FQHCs, and the City Department of Public Health and its FQHC sites and TB program. The health center works with the hospitals, the area MCOs, and recently with the ACO, to follow up with patients who have emergency department visits and inpatients services to find ways to address their needs to avoid frequent ED use and rehospitalization. All of the FQHCs noted on the Map in Attachment 1 have provided letters of support for this application, as have a number of other organizations and civic leaders. There are no rural health clinics serving the FPCSN service area.

children.title: 3. Collaboration with Primary Care Association and Health Center Controlled Network
children.content:
FPCSN is a member of the Pennsylvania Association of Community Health Centers, which works directly with the state Departments of Health and Human Services and the Medical Assistance Office, on behalf of the FQHCs in the state. The FPCSN CEO has been on the PACHC Board of Directors and was Chairperson of the Policy Committee. FPCSN partners with the Association around advocacy for Pennsylvania FQHCs as well as for outreach and enrollment services.
FPCSN is also a member of the Health Federation of Philadelphia and its Health Center Controlled Network. The HCCN helps the health center optimize its use of information technology tools and quality improvement efforts to improve patient care. The health center receives dashboards regarding the quality measures and aggregate health-related social needs

Much of FPCSN's collaborative work takes place through the Health Federation, which sponsors training that all of the FQHCs use, operates joint purchasing services, and facilitates projects such as one to find additional ways to integrate behavioral health with primary care and one that trained medical assistants on advancing their role to improve quality of care outcomes. children.title: 4. Applicants requesting PHPC Funding
children.content:
a/b. How Public Housing Residents Are Involved in Planning and Ongoing Administration The FPCSN health centers have been created in partnership with the communities they serve and their services are reflective of community need and in partnership with the Philadelphia Housing Authority. The collaborative relationship with the Housing Authority has continued through the 33 years the FPCSN has been providing health services to public housing residents. The Network began in the Abbottsford Homes, a tenant-managed community, where the health center has always had a strong relationship with tenant leadership based on mutual respect. A resident of Abbottsford Homes was the President of the health center advisory committee for 26 years. The FPCSN Board members include one member who is a representative of the Housing Authority and two others who are or have been public housing residents.
As noted above, each of the comprehensive FPCSN sites now has a Community Advisory Committee to help the site understand the needs and opinions of the community. The Network Community Engagement Manager facilitates Community Advisory Committee meetings at each site that include patients, including current and former PHA residents, recruited in partnership with PHA Tenant Councils, to ensure diverse community representation. She also leads quarterly patient town halls, creating an efficient communication platform where patients discuss new initiatives, service quality, and emerging needs. Insights from these meetings directly inform programming, outreach, and CQI priorities, as does the Strategic Plan. Through these coordinated strategies, FPCSN stays closely aligned with the needs of residents in Philadelphia Housing Authority communities through a coordinated model of care and community engagement. Through these structures, FPCSN integrates resident feedback into program design, resulting in initiatives like patient access, health education and outreach, perinatal wellness, and food security programs. This collaborative approach ensures that FPCSN services remain responsive, community-led, and aligned with the lived experiences of public housing residents.

sections.title: CAPACITY 1. Organizational Structure
sections.content:
Family Practice & Counseling Services Network, Inc. is a 501(c)3 organization registered in Pennsylvania and listed with the IRS. The structure, management system, and lines of authority are appropriate to the delivery of high-quality care and are fully compliant with Section 330 requirements. As noted on the Organization Chart and Bylaws (Attachments 2 and 3), the organization is governed by a Board of Directors that elects its own members and exists to operate the FPCSN health center. The Board hires and evaluates the Executive Director/CEO. FPCSN has an Executive Team, Senior Leadership Team and Extended Leadership team that manage all aspects of the health center operations.

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
b/c. Subawards and Contractors
sections.content: FPCSN does not have a parent, affiliate, or subsidiary organization. It has no subawards, subrecipients, or contracts with any other organization to perform substantive programmatic work within the scope of project. FPCSN has contracts with the University of Pennsylvania Family Medicine for prenatal and obstetric care and with some other providers, but these are not substantial parts of the provider staff. All contracts and agreements include language indicating that no limit is placed on the health center's authority.

children.title: 2. Management/Executive Team
children.content:
FPCSN has a very experienced Executive Team and the expertise and capabilities to manage the operation and oversight of the health center. All of the managers, including the CEO who is the Project Director, are hired directly. The FPCSN Chief Executive Officer reports to the Board and has responsibility for day-to-day operations, working with the Executive Leadership Team, which meets formally each week and includes the Chief Operations Officer (COO), the Chief Finance Officer (CFO), the Chief Clinical Officer (CD) , the Chief Compliance Officer, the Chief Strategy and Development Officer, and the Chief Human Resources Officer. A Senior Leadership Team that meets bi-weekly also includes the clinical directors for behavioral health, integrated behavioral health, and oral health, and the directors of the sites, and the Chief IT Officer (CIO).
Emily Nichols has been the Executive Director of the health center since 2019. She was Director of Operations for six years and Director of one of the sites for three years before that. Her career has focused on Philadelphia-based FQHCs in a variety of roles centering on patient care, health outcomes, quality improvement, and patient-centered medical home recognition. She holds a BA in Public Health/Social and Behavioral Sciences from Johns Hopkins University and an MPH from Emory University with a concentration in Behavioral Sciences. Emily is dedicated to quality programming driven by population need and strives for outcomes that meet those needs in addressing the health-related social needs that can prevent engagement in health care services.
Aaron Chavis is the FPCSN Chief Operations Officer. He holds a BA in Law and Justice from Rowan University and a Master of Public Administration from Florida State University and he has worked in the capacity of Interim Executive Director and Vice President of Operations over his career. Aaron manages FPCSN operations with the Center Directors and over 150 employees to ensure all programming is uniform and in keeping with the agency's principals and mission. The position of Chief Clinical Officer is vacant and the Clinical Director position is being filled on an interim basis by Jennifer Cohn, MD, from the Penn Department of Family Medicine and Community Health. She has provided obstetric and collaborative physician services for FPCSN for a number of years. The Chief Financial Officer position is held by Marcus Freeman who is a seasoned healthcare executive with more than twenty years of leadership experience within FQHCs and community-based healthcare organizations. The Chief Information Officer is Cheryl Rodenfels, who is also a consultant. She brings almost 20 years of IT service management and leadership, much of it in healthcare organizations.
a. Management Team Support and Oversight of the Health Center
The FPCSN Executive and Leadership Teams provide robust support for the operation and oversight of the health center and all of its programs by establishing a framework for policy implementation, accountability, and risk management. Regular supervision occurs for all staff with regular team meetings. To ensure accountability, the health center management uses the

sections.content: quality improvement process to implement a performance monitoring system that tracks key metrics related to patient care, financial stability, and operational efficiency. Comprehensive policies and procedures are followed and will be regularly reviewed to ensure compliance with federal, state, and local regulations. The Executive and Leadership Teams proactively address potential challenges through a structured risk management plan, which includes identifying, assessing, and mitigating risks to protect the program's integrity and long-term viability.

sections.title: b. Promotion of Innovation and Quality Improvement
sections.content:
The FPCSN Executive Team promotes innovation and a culture of quality improvement and has worked to improve patient satisfaction, make technology enhancements to increase access to care, review staff compensation packages to be competitive and support staff retention, and enhance financial sustainability through added services and other funding opportunities. The team actively engages with the community to identify needs and develop responsive solutions, in particular through partnership with PACHC and the Health Federation and its HCCN. This engagement is also achieved through regular community needs assessments, patient satisfaction surveys, and staff feedback mechanisms. At FPCSN, innovations most often come from clinicians or other staff and they are always focused on improving the quality of health or life for patients and the community served. The Executive Team works with the Quality Improvement Committee that is tasked with analyzing data, piloting revised care models, and implementing evidence-based practices. Innovation has been fostered by allocating resources for pilot projects and encouraging an innovation mentality, where lessons learned from unsuccessful initiatives are used to inform future efforts. By creating an environment where feedback is valued and new ideas are encouraged, the Executive Team works to ensure the health center remains adaptable, effective, and truly responsive to the evolving needs of the community it serves.
The FPCSN Strategic Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Plan promote innovation and continuous quality improvement in the organization. These frameworks guide organizational priorities, align quality goals with community needs, and ensure evidence-based decision-making across all departments. Accountability for quality and innovation is embedded at every level. The FPCSN Board of Directors provides governance and oversees strategic direction and outcomes; the Executive Leadership Team provides strategic direction and ensures alignment throughout the Network; and the Senior Leadership Team (Department Leaders) manages implementation of performance and quality initiatives. The Extended Leadership Team that includes leaders across all FPCSN sites, meets monthly to review Network data, progress, and operational improvements. The CQI Committee meets quarterly to evaluate outcomes, monitor metrics, and update the CQI Plan. FPCSN's CQI process integrates patient feedback, survey data, and Community Advisory Committee input to inform service enhancements and pilot innovative care models. This structured, data-driven approach fosters accountability, transparency, and cross- departmental collaboration, ensuring innovation and quality remain central to achieving equitable, high-performing, and patient-centered care.

children.title: 3. Recruitment and Retention of Qualified Providers
children.content:
When recruitment for management or provider positions is necessary, FPCSN uses all available avenues and tools to find the most qualified people and people who are motivated to work with the underserved patients and communities FPCSN serves. The health center assures that potential candidates are aware of the advantages of working for the Network, including the mentoring they will receive from the experienced leadership and providers, the opportunities they will have to
31

sections.content:
participate in a nurse-managed model of care and with staff in a transdisciplinary model, the possibilities for flexible scheduling, and the funds available to support ongoing education.
To encourage retention of managers and staff, FPCSN tries to pay salaries that are competitive in the market and offer an innovative plan of benefits, including a generous PTO package. Staff receive orientation and extensive training and continuing education, allowing them to earn Continuing Education credits. Clinical staff are provided time and reimbursement for conference expenses as the budget allows. Staff attend regular meetings that include case conferences and problem solving and support sessions, serve on committees such as CQI, and represent FPCSN on local and national committees. These activities ensure staff are as involved as possible in health center operations and decision-making to maximize their investment in the program and the work they and their colleagues are doing. FPCSN has had a recent initiative around the prevention of burn out and promotion of self-care, through which Mind-Body staff are available to staff as well as patients.

children.title: 4. Financial Accounting and Control Systems
children.content: In order to maintain its high degree of financial accuracy, FPCSN employs a solid financial management system. To assure that Generally Accepted Accounting Principles are followed, exacting internal controls are utilized, in that: 1) fiscal procedures are developed, continually updated, and stringently followed; 2) budgets are properly developed, approved, reviewed, and managed by a fiscal team; 3) all financial transactions are properly recorded and fully disclosed to all relevant parties; 4) all administrative costs are properly documented and periodically reviewed for their accuracy; 5) a professional audit of all fiscal matters is performed annually by a recognized outside auditing firm and reviewed by the FPCSN Board of Directors.

sections.title: a. Control Over Health Center Funds
sections.content: The FPCSN Chief Financial Officer acts to ensure that the activities of the health center are managed appropriately, run as efficiently as possible, are staffed appropriately, and ensure that GAAP standards can be maintained and FPCSN policies and procedures are followed. When necessary, the CFO recommends the need for policy and/or procedure change when that change enhances the efficiency of the department. The CFO works to ensure that the Financial Policies and Procedures meet all Federal, State, Government and Private guidelines, including federal payment management system requirements. This includes policies and procedures as they relate to grant funding as well as third party billing. FPCSN Policies specifically address IRS Filing, Audit, Insurance Coverage, Procurement, Internal Controls, Fee Schedules and Discounts, Billing and Collections, Budget and Accounting, Grants Management and staff travel. Accounting procedures maintain effective control over, and accountability for, all funds, property, and other assets.

sections.title: b. Tracking of Financial Performance
sections.content:
FPCSN's accounting procedures maintain effective control over, and accountability for, all funds, property, and other assets and are in full compliance with federal, state and local regulations. They include the following specific accounting areas: check requisitions, invoices, accounts payable, accounts receivable, inventory, leases, cash receipts, payroll, benefits, and insurance. There is a comprehensive chart of accounts, ranging from wages and salaries to staff travel to property repair. Separate units have been established for each health center and clinical department, allowing revenue and expenses to be identified and directly charged to related programs. Effective segregation of duties represents a vital component to the organization's
32

sections.content:
internal control system. Segregation of duties controls are designed and reviewed as an internal control to help prevent employees from committing errors or engaging in fraudulent activity. The system is under the supervision of the CFO who has long experience in complying with general accounting procedures. The system is sufficient to assure that all health center revenues are used solely for authorized purposes.
FPCSN financial management activities are centralized, computerized, and readily accessible for audit and review. FPCSN utilizes AthenaPractice and VisDental practice management software, which have reports on revenue by source, aged accounts receivable by source, provider productivity, and encounters by payor category, among many other financial reports. In addition, monthly general ledger financial and budget reports allow management to analyze the organization's financial standing and trends. These systems allow FPCSN to enter and process demographic, clinical, and billing data. Patients can be identified by age, gender, zip code, income eligibility, family size, clinical services provided, insurance payer, and other demographics. Both practice management systems allow billed and actual payment to be reconciled. It also provides an accurate aged account receivable given the reimbursement processes of some organization funders. This system is key to many areas of patient billing as a threshold can be selected to initiate a follow-up or action. The system allows denials, cash payment cycles and other key operating indicators to be monitored. FPCSN is able to download billing and account receivable information directly into the current general ledger system to allow the finance department to perform reconciliations in a consistent and timely manner.

sections.title: c. Accounting for a 330 Award and Other Awards and Funds
sections.content: The financial accounting and internal control systems assure that all federal and other funds are fully accounted for, including maintaining source documentation, that all expenditures of funds are allowable, which will include Section 330 and other federal awards, and that only actual work performed is charged to each funding source. Funds received are tracked in separate general ledger accounts. Salaries and wages and other non-personnel costs supported by any funds are recorded in these separate general ledger accounts.

sections.title: d. Mitigation of Conflict of Interest
sections.content:
To assure that decisions are free of bias, interest, or personal benefit or gain on the part of any member of the Board of Directors, Executive Team, or any other staff, FPCSN has a Conflict of Interests Policy, which states that "No Board member, officer, employee, or agent shall participate in FPCSN's selection, award, or administration of any contract or grant when a real or apparent conflict of interest is involved". The policy states that "For purposes of contracting, a 'Conflict of Interest' arises when an employee, officer, director or agent, any member of his, her or their immediate family, his/her/their partner or an organization which employs or is about to employ any of those parties, has a financial, fiduciary or other interest in, and/or derives a tangible personal benefit from a firm considered by FPCSN for a contract". The policy details the process by which conflicts will be determined and the process of recusal when a conflict exists. All Board members and staff must sign a Conflict Reporting Form at least annually confirming any conflicts of interest they may have or their non-conflicting status. The Conflict of Interests Policy is referenced in the FPCSN Procurement Policy that requires that all procurement transactions should be conducted in a manner to provide open and free competition and in the FPCSN Bylaws.

children.title: 5. Planning for Disasters and Emergencies
children.content: FPCSN has an Emergency Preparedness and Response Plan that outlines the health center's response to a disaster, including ways that patients would continue to receive care; how FPCSN would integrate its response with those of City agencies, area hospitals, and other medical providers; and how health center providers could be deployed to other organizations. An Emergency Response Team includes professional medical staff such as medical assistants, nurses and nurse practitioners, behavioral health staff such as therapists and crisis specialists, and some administrative personnel. The health centers will not be points of distribution for antibiotics, vaccines, and medical supplies; rather, staff will be deployed to city sites to assist in distribution. FPCSN will work with the City to integrate with its preparedness, conducting annual drills in coordination with the city if needed. FPCSN will have a plan for an emergency situation that is coordinated with the City's plan. The health center has insurance to prevent financial revenue and facilities loss in an emergency, as well as downtime procedures for each clinical department to ensure we can provide services in emergency situations. The Pennsylvania Forum for Primary Health Emergency Preparedness Manual, which is extensive, is the basis for the FPCSN Plan.

children.title: 6. Factors Affecting Three-Year Trend
children.content: The UDS reports filed over the last three years when the health center was operating as an FQHC indicated that the health center served 14,872 patients in CY2022, 15,214 patients in CY2023, and 15,617 patients in CY 2024. So, the trend has been upward since the pandemic. However, in the last twelve months, because of the upheaval and uncertainty of the separation from RHD and loss of FQHC status, many providers and other staff have left to seek positions elsewhere. As a result, further growth in the next year is not being projected in this application but as new staff are hired, FPCSN will increase the number of patients served.

children.title: 1. Quality Improvement and Quality Assurance Program
children.content:
The FPCSN quality improvement process is led by a Continuous Quality Improvement Committee that is headed by the Quality Manager and includes the CEO, the Primary Care Director and the Chief Clinical Officer, and the Department Heads from Integrated Behavioral Health, Outpatient Behavioral Health, Dental, Human Resources, and Finance. The CQI Committee is charged with enhancing the organization's performance by setting benchmarks, measuring progress made toward those goals, determining and implementing improvement plans when necessary.
The purview of the CQI Committee includes four areas:
· Oversight and compliance, including policies and protocols, staff credentials, the risk management program, emergency training, and the peer review process;
· Quality Improvement and Quality Assurance, including internal controls and procedures, reports on performance on quality measures, identifying and analyzing areas identified for improvement, support of PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) improvement cycles, and tracking patient and staff satisfaction;
· Pay for Performance, Incentives, and Productivity; and
· Financial Metrics.
The Quality Manager, in partnership with Department Directors, is responsible for assuring that all staff, as well as Board and Site Advisory Committee members, are trained in the CQI process.

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
a. Follows Clinical Guidelines and Standards of Care
sections.content:
As noted above, FPCSN is recognized as a Patient Centered Medical Home. The health center has extensive operating procedures and clinical protocols in place that further this recognition. Evidence based guidelines and Clinical Decision Support tools are embedded in the electronic medical record, so that providers and staff receive prompts for patient care based on their diagnoses and services they provide. FPCSN has protocols in place regarding the care of obesity, diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular health and risk reduction, cancer screening, screening for depression and behavioral health, prenatal care, women's health, infant immunization, and asthma. The Azara DRVS and i2iTracks population management software provides data reports regarding providers' adherence to these protocols and the outcomes for their patients in dashboards that include individual practitioner, clinic site, and health center results. These are used in supervision, site and health center management, and in the aggregate by the Board for decision making regarding the health center.
The FPCSN Quality Improvement and Risk Management Procedures indicate the processes to be conducted to maintain and continually improve the quality of care provided by the health centers. The providers conduct audits of each other's charts bi-annually, under the peer review procedure, during which they review practice performance for completeness of charting, accuracy of coding, and documentation of preventive care and activities satisfying quality of care measures as defined by the Network as well as HEDIS measures and other external benchmarks.

sections.title: b. Addresses Patient Safety
sections.content: FPCSN has an established incident reporting system whereby personnel are required to bring to the attention of appropriate administrative staff any incident that affects or threatens to affect the health and/or safety of any person associated with the health centers. Procedures exist that detail any external parties that must be notified, such as for suspected child abuse or neglect, and how such notice should occur. Incidents reported are reviewed in the CQI Committee. Adverse incidents are reviewed by the Primary Care Director and the Executive Director, along with other managers and supervisory staff appropriate to the situation. Incidents provide information to be used in revision of processes, training of staff, and other actions that will increase the level of safety for patients and staff. All incidents reported are reviewed quarterly by the CQI committee and are assigned a red, yellow, or green status to indicate the severity of the incident. Sentinel events are incidents that result in harm, injury, or death to a patient or a person associated with the health centers. Sentinel events are investigated by the Primary Care Director and a thorough chart review is conducted with other clinical leaders if the affected person is a patient.

sections.title: Risk Management
sections.content:
FPCSN has policies and procedures that govern its risk management processes, including those related to facility maintenance and inspections, monitoring of OSHA requirements, incident and accident response and review, fire and emergency drills, confidentiality and HIPAA compliance, and credentialing and privileging procedures. Each site has a written plan for dealing with fire and other life-threatening situations that details evacuation procedures, including how children or persons with disabilities will be assisted. The plans also indicate how management will be notified and the plan of action in case a site must be closed as a result of the crisis. Fire drills are held quarterly in each site, with at least one per year held when regular services for clients are being held. The CQI process monitors these processes and implements any quality improvement projects needed to assure that patients and staff are as safe as possible and that risk is minimized.

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
sections.content: FPCSN procedures cover employees' responsibility to notice and report any potential hazards in the health centers. Quarterly inspections are done by the Compliance Manager in each site to detect unsafe conditions, initiate preventive maintenance, and monitor actions on work required. Such inspections cover all aspects of facility safety, including OSHA, Department of Health, Fire Department, and ADA requirements and any other laws and regulations, including those requiring proper signage in the centers. Plumbing, ventilation, heating, cooling, lighting, fire extinguishers, and alarm systems are all included in the inspection process. Any problems discovered during the inspection process are addressed as quickly as possible. Any preventive maintenance needed is also noted and completed. All staff are expected to take all necessary safety precautions when operating health center equipment or securing the premises. Smoking is not permitted in the health centers or when staff are escorting patients or during outreach work.

sections.title: c. Improves Patient Experience and Satisfaction
sections.content:
The FPCSN QI Plan indicates that annual patient satisfaction surveys will be conducted, but these actually take place much more often. Plans are underway to use the patient portal to conduct brief surveys after every visit. The results of these surveys are reviewed in the CQI Committee, which develops any needed quality improvement projects. The results of the surveys and resulting actions are shared with the site advisory committees, shared with health center staff, and put on the website to assure patients that the surveys are used and their voices are being heard. FPCSN has a Patients' Complaints and Grievances Policy that affirms patients' rights to voice grievances regarding their care, without recrimination, and to have those reviewed and, when possible, resolved. The procedure indicates that staff should encourage patients to make any issues they have with their care known and outlines how grievances are handled, including final decisions by the Executive Director and access to grievance mechanisms at the state and HRSA levels.
d. Uses Electronic Health Record and Population Management System to Track Risk Factors FPCSN incorporated Centricity into the practice as an EHR and practice management system in 2005. The organization has regularly transitioned to more recent versions of the Centricity Practice Solutions system, converting the EHR to the certified meaningful use version. FPCSN system is now known as AthenaPractice. It is an integrated EHR and practice management system that fully supports the CQI process and is used to track clinical and financial data, providing information on both the clinical outcome measures for all life cycles and the financial goals of the program. The EHR has evidence-based practice standards embedded in the clinical decision support tool, so that those standards, as well as others that the provider staff chooses, can be used for CQI. The Dental Department uses VisDental, a Visualutions product that embeds into AthenaPractice so FPCSN has one EHR for patients using most of its services. Efforts are currently underway to bring behavioral health services into the EHR. This will allow seamless, integrated care for all patients accessing services along with one system for all reporting.
The FPCSN AthenaPractice system contains a complete login and securitylaccess system to meet all HIPAA requirements; integrated MQIC reporting functionality and analysis for Meaningful Use patient care analysis; and reporting, patient registration, scheduling, billing and medical chart tracking in all ambulatory and primary care and behavioral health areas. It is backed by integrated quality reporting metrics, and serves as a self-contained, HIPAA-compliant and Meaningful Use certified product.

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
sections.content:
Data collected is provided to the CQI Committee, which analyzes the information and makes plans for clinical and fiscal improvement. FPCSN uses Azara DRVS and i2iTracks, population management software that is directly connected to AthenaPractice. It is a unidirectional interface where EHR data feeds directly into Azara DRVS and i2iTracks, a population management software that is directly connected to AthenaPractice. Within Azara and i2iTracks, there is a host of data functionality:
· Huddle reports that the primary care teams review daily that give an overview of patients scheduled for the day, clinical items due (cervical cancer screenings, vaccines due, etc.), protocols (ie, consider BHC visit for uncontrolled hypertension, or metformin for prediabetes)
· Patient searches - list of patients with whatever criteria/filters are needed for functions such as recall or to create a tracking type, such as high-risk.
· Reporting - all quality reporting for primary care and integrated behavioral health
· Referral and Lab tracking
· All UDS reporting, including UDS+ reporting through Azara
As noted above, data is used by the CQI Committee, health center managers, and the board to design service improvements and guide decision-making.
FPCSN collects data regarding health-related social needs from patients, using the Phreesia tool on tablet/iPads at intake for each visit for patient registration and to screen for depression and other mental health needs, substance use, and violence and other HRSN issues, such as any need for legal assistance or HIV testing, problems with access to an internet-connected device or Internet access, and a digital literacy assessment. FPCSN runs reports on what the patient population is experiencing and gaps in resources and uses this information to assure that services are meeting patient needs.

children.title: 2. Improvement of Clinical Quality and Health Outcomes
children.content: FPCSN is focused on the quality of the services provided to its patient population and on improving the health of the population of the area served. Standards for treatment for a number of chronic conditions are embedded in the EHR and preventive and treatment services and outcomes are reviewed through peer reviews and individual provider dashboards.

sections.title: a. Body Mass Index (BMI) Screening and Follow-Up Plan
sections.content:
The population being served by FPCSN has a number of health-related social needs that are barriers to the maintenance of a healthy weight. The population largely lives in poverty, has a high prevalence of obesity, and lacks knowledge about and access to healthy food and many live in areas where it is unsafe to spend time outdoors exercising. FPCSN has an Overweight and Obesity in Adults Clinical Practice Protocol to give provider and patient teams need guidance and support to assure that medical care and follow up consistently follow evidence-based guidelines. The Protocol includes the requirement that all patients with BMI > 30 with or without co-morbidities are defined as high risk and referred to the RN Care Manager, the Behavioral Health Consultant, and the Nutritionist/Diabetic Educator who provides the nutrition/physical activity counseling. Patients with a BMI > 25 with co-morbidities are defined as moderate risk and referred to the above support staff as needed plus provided the nutrition/physical activity counseling. All BMIs > 25 and BMIs < 18.5 or 22 (per age group) are incorporated into the obesity-overweight/underweight dashboard and individual provider reports are provided to the providers on a quarterly basis to allow recall for any risk category as needed.

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
sections.content: Many patients have difficulty following through with their care plans and need additional counseling and support to follow the regime to manage their weight. The Nutritionist and/or diabetic educator make outreach/recall efforts with patients with moderate or high risk who have not been seen in at least a quarter. They are encouraged to come in for care and ongoing counseling regarding their care plans. Healthy eating is a priority for FPCSN. One site initiated a Food Access in the Community Team (FACT) group and partnered with Philabundance to become a food distribution site and now food pantries and WIC are on-site at each of the three comprehensive sites to help address food insecurity.

sections.title: b. Breast, Cervical, and Colorectal Cancer Screenings
sections.content:
FPCSN was awarded Accelerating Cancer Screening (AxCS) funding in 2024 to increase colorectal cancer screening and referral in collaboration with the Spectrum FQHC, the Philadelphia Health Federation and its HCCN, and the Penn Abramson Cancer Center. The partnering organizations are focusing on unscreened patients of the participating centers, including over five thousand Philadelphia residents, who experience multiple barriers to health care services. Through centralized use of population health management systems, the project team developed outreach lists for mailings that include patient education and a FIT kit, thus enabling a broad, efficient approach to addressing gaps in preventive screening. Text messages informed by behavioral science encourage patients to use the FIT kits and mail back their tests. The health centers' systems are being used to track and follow-up on results. The Cancer Center supports the health centers with expertise in mailed FIT campaigns as well as through a contracted Navigator who provides intensive, personalized services to patients who have a positive FIT and need colonoscopy and possible follow-up care. Through this methodology, the partnering organizations expect to double rates of colorectal cancer screening. The project is testing the viability of centralized, technology-enabled outreach for cancer screening, in order to support enhanced capacity for navigation services in FQHCs.
FPCSN has a Women's Health Clinical Practice Protocol to give provider and patient teams guidance and support to assure that medical care and follow up consistently follow evidence- based guidelines. The Protocol includes a workflow that dictates all women with appointments for family planning who have not had timely Pap tests or mammograms will have Pap tests set up in the exam room before the provider comes in. Those who need mammograms will receive a referral to their imaging center of choice. FPCSN has a Women's Health team, consisting of a women's health NP and a medical assistant, who recall women who have not come in for Pap tests as needed and rotate among the Philadelphia sites to conduct these. Patients often resist these tests and do not understand the importance of screening. Since the time recommended between tests has increased, some patients forget about them. Patients who are due for testing receive outreach/recall messages to schedule tests. FPCSN is learning from the formal work being done around colorectal screening and will apply that learning to Pap testing as well.

sections.title: c. Diabetes and Hypertension Control
sections.content:
The population being served by FPCSN has a number of health-related social needs that are barriers to the maintenance of a healthy weight and the management of diabetes and hypertension. The population largely lives in poverty, has a high prevalence of obesity, and lacks knowledge about the dangers of their condition. There is also a lack of knowledge about advances in diabetic treatment such as weekly injections that can help patients more easily manage their disease. It has also been demonstrated that stress can contribute to poor
38

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
sections.content:
cardiovascular health and increased blood pressure. FPCSN is committed to being a pioneer at the forefront of identifying and amplifying effective community-level interventions for diabetes and blood pressure control. FPCSN is working to address social needs as part of its approach to controlling these conditions and educating patients on the impact they have on physical health.
FPCSN has a number of resources available to assist individuals in need of help in controlling their weight, their blood pressure, or their HbA1c levels. The FPCSN Clinical Practice Protocols for Diabetes and Hypertension give provider and patient teams guidance and support to assure that medical care and follow up consistently follow evidence-based guidelines. A "Diabetes Distress Scale" is used to assess barriers patients are facing in controlling their diabetes and tailor interventions to meet their needs. BHC involvement has increased with uncontrolled diabetics who have co-morbid behavioral health needs. Guidelines detail counseling to be provided by the medical providers and when patients are referred to the Nutritionist, Diabetes Educator, and/or BHC staff. Patients are treated by a team that helps them develop and carry out care plans and services may be extended to their homes and communities through CHW staff. Likewise, patients with blood pressure over 180/110 with or without co- morbidities are defined as high risk and referred to the RN Care Manager to identify barriers to care, and to the BHC and Nutritionist/Educator for services. All diabetic and hypertensive patients are incorporated into the dashboard and reports are generated for the providers quarterly to allow recall for any risk category as needed. Any patient who has not had follow-up within six months, or three months for moderate risk, receives outreach messages to encourage them to come in for care. These efforts have been expanded to all three comprehensive sites and to prenatal patients as well.
FPCSN has been part of a national pilot program with the Million Hearts initiative, focused specifically on reduction of blood pressure in the African American community, to pilot a community-level intervention focusing on understanding of hypertension risks and how to prevent cardiovascular events. Healthy eating is a priority for FPCSN. One site initiated a Food Access in the Community Team (FACT) group and partnered with Philabundance to become a food distribution site and now food pantries and WIC are on-site at each of the three comprehensive sites to help address food insecurity.

sections.title: d. Screening for Depression and Follow-Up Planning
sections.content:
FPCSN has had behavioral health counseling in place since the inception of the health center and has been a pioneer in the integration of behavioral health services with primary care. FPCSN has a Depression Screening Clinical Practice Protocol in place to give provider and patient teams guidance and support to assure that care and follow up consistently follow evidence-based guidelines. Behavioral Health Consultant services are embedded in primary care to provide additional assessment, brief intervention, and referral to more intensive services. Psychiatric Nurse Practitioners are also embedded in primary care at our three comprehensive sites for patients who are in need of medication management. Patients can be referred by these BH providers or their primary care provider to FPCSN Outpatient located within the three comprehensive sites and will soon be available in the Mill Creek site as well. Outpatient services are provided by clinical psychologists, licensed professional counselors, psychiatric nurse practitioners, and licensed clinical social workers, as well as psychiatrists.
Screening for depression, other mental health issues, and substance use issues is completed on tablets every six months as part of the FPCSN registration system. This has significantly increased the number of patients screened and identified for intervention. Managers are working to

sections.title: Family Practice & Counseling Services Network 2026 Service Area Competition Application
sections.content: proactively identify patients screening positively on the tablets so that they provide an intervention during the same days' visit. The medical assistants are trained to be aware of patients' use or reluctance to use the questions on the intake screening and to respond appropriately. Most outpatient behavioral health services are being provided remotely at this time and the health center has found that this meets the needs of a number of patients, addressing the reluctance of some to use mental health services because of stigma or concerns about confidentiality. Some patients are more comfortable using these services while in their own environment.

sections.title: e. Tobacco Use Screening and Cessation Intervention
sections.content:
FPCSN has a Tobacco Screening and Cessation Clinical Practice Protocol to give provider and patient teams need guidance and support to assure that medical care and follow up consistently follow evidence-based guidelines. The Clinical Practice Protocol that creates a consistent approach in screening/cessation and prompts MAs to screen tobacco use during triage/intake. They set out literature about cessation counseling when a positive screen is found and thus prompt provider completion of cessation counseling.
f. Weight Assessment/Counseling for Nutrition and Physical Activity for Children and Adolescents Many families served by FPCSN have difficulty helping their children to follow through with their care plans and need additional counseling and support. The nurse practitioners in primary care provide children whose weight is above or below a healthy level with counseling about healthy nutrition and physical activity. FPCSN has an Overweight and Obesity in Children/Adolescents Clinical Practice Protocol to give provider and patient teams need guidance and support to assure that medical care and follow up consistently follow evidence- based guidelines. A Nutritionist and/or diabetic educator may make outreach/recall efforts with patients with moderate or high risk who have not been seen in at least a quarter. They will be encouraged to come in for care and ongoing counseling regarding their care plans.

children.title: 1. Governing Board Reflection of the Community and Engagement with Patients
children.content: As noted in Form 6A, 50% of the 12 members of the FPCSN Board of Directors are current users of the health center's services. One member is a representative of the Housing Authority and two others are or have been public housing residents. Most of the members live and/or work in the service area and several have expertise in community engagement. The patient Board members report on their experiences as patients, from the time they walk into the health center and encounter the person registering them through checkout, allowing management and fellow members to get accurate feedback on patient appointments. All of the Board members are active members of the community and they seek out people in the area to assure they are aware of the health center and to learn of any concerns there might be about services provided. Those who are not patients share their experiences and the opinions they gather outside of the health center.

sections.title: a. The Board Leverages Members' Expertise
sections.content:
All but one of the FPCSN board members live and/or work in the area served and they include individuals with expertise in law, public relations, education, community engagement, and healthcare operations and strategy. The Board members are eager to share their expertise and their input is welcomed by management as well as their fellow Board members. The Board's varying expertise will guide FPCSN strategic direction through program design, community

sections.content:
engagement, and public health initiatives. At each Board meeting, there is a discussion of sharing resources among board members that could be utilized at the health centers.
b. The Board Provides Oversight and Strategic Direction
The FPCSN Bylaws indicate the Board is responsible for strategic planning and monitoring of program progress on those plans. The needs assessment of the service area community and the ongoing collaboration with the Housing Authority and other community partners are used by the Board for strategic planning. The information provided to the Board for strategic planning includes information on the health center's performance improvement activities, which include utilization and outcomes data. The members of the Board who are users of FPCSN services and those with connections in the community bring valuable information to this process. The Board utilizes the strategic plan as the blueprint for increasing and improving service to the community. The review of progress toward strategic plan goals, along with the reports from the CQI process, are mechanisms for the Board's use in its ongoing monitoring of organizational services, meeting the needs of the community, and patient satisfaction.
c. The Board Evaluates Financial and Quality Performance
The FPCSN Bylaws indicate the Board is responsible to evaluate the quality of the work of the health center and provide for organizational stability and compliance. The Board approves the fiscal year budget and CQI plan yearly. Quality Improvement reports, including progress on clinical and financial performance measures, incidents, and patient and staff satisfaction, are made to the board quarterly. The Board receives regular updates on the CQI process, which allows them to be aware of patient outcomes and satisfaction and any incidents that pose risk to the organization. The FPCSN management and the Board work closely together to manage the finances of the health center. The fiscal management policies and procedures have been approved by the Board and are reviewed annually. Monthly reports are provided to the CEO and are reviewed during regular management meetings and Board meetings. This financial information is used for decision-making regarding programs, staffing, and monitoring of the Financial Performance measures.

sections.title: SUPPORT REQUESTED
sections.content:
Complete budget materials for the year of the project period are included in this application, as are the staffing and income analysis forms. The budget assumptions are reasonable, given the number of users and the number of billable visits, as well as the severity of the health problems experienced by the people being served. The number of patients projected to be served is the same as the number listed as the SAAT patient target. The budgeted expenses, including salaries, benefits, and other supply and administrative costs, are reasonable for the size of the health center and the volume and types of services to be provided. Salaries and fringes represent half of the overall expenses. FPCSN is operated in a way that maximizes non-grant revenue and leverages other resources, particularly patient service fees and 340B funds, for health center operations. The percentage of federal grant dollars in the overall budget has always been reasonable and was 19% of the budget in the most recent year.
